Summary of Hannah Arendt - The Human Condition
Could you guys point me into the direction of a good summary of Hannah Arendt's the Human Condition. I have an exam on political philosophy soonish and that is one of the books to read. Other book is Sheldon Wolin - Politics and Vision: Continuity and Innovation in Western Political Thought (expanded edition). So in case there is a summary for that then I'd be much obliged! title: What did Hannah Arendt really mean by the banality of evil?
excerpts:
Section Title: What did Hannah Arendt really mean by the banality of evil?
Content:
i
by Thomas White + BIO
Thomas White is a Wiley Journal contributing author, whose philosophical and theological writings have appeared in print and online.
Edited by Sam Dresser
1,400 words Save
Published in association with [American Philosophical Association](https://www.apaonline.org/) , an Aeon Partner
REPUBLISH FOR FREE
Email Save Post Share
Adolf Eichmann at his 1961 trial. *Photo courtesy Wikipedia*
Can one *do* evil without *being* evil? This was the puzzling question that the philosopher Hannah Arendt grappled with when she reported for *The New Yorker* in 1961 on the war crimes trial of Adolph Eichmann, the Nazi operative responsible for organising the transportation of millions of Jews and others to various concentration camps in support of the Nazi’s Final Solution.

title: The Origins of Totalitarianism by Hannah Arendt - Penguin Books Australia

Content:
Her masterpiece ... Arendt's inquiry into the elements of totalitarian domination teaches us we must never let go of the fear of totalitarian governmentLos Angeles Review of Books
Her greatest work is this 1951 classic ... More than any thinker it was Hannah Arendt who identified how those movements of ideas, racial theories, people and methods take place, showing how they fused with other forces - most notably European antisemitism - to shape and ultimately disfigure the twentieth centuryDavid Olusoga, Guardian
Show all

title: Hannah Arendt: The Origins of Totalitarianism. Chapter 1: Antisemitism as an Outrage to Common Sense – Jenny Connected
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > 3 thoughts on “ Hannah Arendt: The Origins of Totalitarianism. Chapter 1: Antisemitism as a...
Content:
I’m afraid I don’t know enough about her work to say.As to your last point about scapegoating, I think I may have to wait until the final chapters of the book to be able to comment on this.What I am struck by at this early stage in reading her book is that Arendt herself was a Jew, who narrowly escaped the death camps. Despite this she was able to stand back and critically look for an explanation/understanding for the terrible events that occurred between 1932/33 and 1945 in Germany and Europe. She was trying to understand what circumstances/chain of events could lead to a totalitarian regime.

***The Origins of Totalitarianism*** , published in 1951, was Hannah Arendt 's first major work, where she describes and analyzes Nazism and Stalinism as the major totalitarian political movements of the first half of the 20th century.
